title: Neglected Risks in the Communication of Residential Mortgage-Backed Securities Offerings
abstract: Examining the contractual disclosures during the sale of private-label residential mortgage-backed securities before the 2008 financial crisis, we find that textual contents in the risk-factor section predict subsequent losses and yet were not reflected in pricing. Insurance companies, especially life insurers and insurers with low regulatory capital ratios, are more exposed to textual risks. Consistent with issuers hedging litigation risks with disclosure, we find that textual contents are associated with second-lien underreporting and preissuance written communications. Overall, we find that investors neglected risks in the purportedly safe assets before the crisis.

article.introduction:
The Great Financial Crisis (GFC)has taught us many lessons. From misaligned incentives of financial intermediaries to overly optimistic expectations about housing prices, researchers have proposed various causes of the boom and bust in the housing markets.1Adelino, Schoar, and Severino (2018) survey the existing evidence and find that the main driver was inflated house price expectations of both households and banks. The GFC also occurred after a period of significant financial innovation. One emblematic example is the private-label residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) market. Investors in this market are typically financial institutions with long investment horizons. Why would these investors believe that the dramatic housing boom could persist? Theoretical studies (e.g., Gennaioli, Shleifer, and Vishny (2012,2015)) argue that financial innovation together with investor neglect of risk led to a fragile market for financial securities. This paper provides evidence on which investors neglected risk before the crisis and why.
  Identifying the source of neglected risks is empirically challenging because it requires that researchers observe the information set available to investors prior to purchasing RMBS. Our study overcomes this challenge by constructing a merged sample of institutional investors' RMBS holdings and the final prospectus supplements at issuance. Since RMBS have no business or active management functions, information in these contractual disclosures is key to evaluating the risks of these securities. We collect final prospectus supplements and any preceding written communications for private-label RMBS deals that went through the public offering process between 2003 and 2007. Our study provides novel empirical evidence on whether investors availed themselves of this crucial source of information.
  To illustrate that information contained in issuance disclosures is risk-relevant, we reproduce the risk-factor descriptions in AppendixA. As the second point states, “The loans were made to borrowers who are not able, or do not wish, to obtain financing from traditional sources. These mortgage loans may be considered to be of a riskier nature than by traditional sources of financing, so that the holders of the certificates may be deemed to be a greater risk of loss.” The fourth point further states that “borrowers have increasingly financed their homes with new mortgage loan products, which in many cases have allowed them to purchase homes that they might otherwise not have been able to afford. … There is little historical data with respect to these new mortgage loan products. Consequently, … borrower delinquencies and defaults could exceed anticipated levels. In that event, the certificates may not perform as you anticipate.” These statements are not boilerplate text in RMBS issuance, but rather clear warnings about the nontraditional features pervasive in private-label securitization prior to the GFC.2Accordingly, they help shed light on the context in which nontraditional sources of financing and new mortgage products experienced defaults beyond historical norms.
  To extract risk-relevant information from final prospectus supplements, we employ textual analysis of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) filings.3Specifically, we parse the text in final prospectus supplements, identifying and calculating the use of uncertain text from lists of uncertain, weak modal, and negative words (Loughran and McDonald (2011)).4Some of the most frequently used uncertain words are “may,” “could,” “loss,” and “risk.”
  The use of uncertain text allows us to examine the extent and intensity of downside scenarios, as illustrated in the examples quoted above. Uncertain words always appear when a separate item is discussed in the risk-factor section, so their frequency reflects the extent of risk factors. In addition, the more an item is emphasized, the greater the number of uncertain words, and thus the use of uncertain words also reflects the severity of potential losses.5To identify the source of variation and to help interpret the use of uncertain words, we examine the contents of four key sections of the final prospectus supplement—risk factor, description of certificates, mortgage pool, and summary—to construct separate measure of uncertain-text use for each section.
  We first investigate the relation between the use of uncertain words and subsequent losses for both collateral and securities. Controlling for an extensive list of characteristics and underwriter and time fixed effects, we find that the use of uncertain words in the risk-factor section is significantly associated with ex post losses for principals, for both collateral and securities. For example, a one-standard-deviation increase in the use of uncertain words in the risk-factor section increases the ex post loss for principals by 22% for collateral and by 25% for AAA-rated tranches, relative to the sample averages. This result holds when we extend the sample to all investment-grade tranches. However, investors do not receive higher yields commensurate with higher potential security losses, and the initial yield spreads for AAA-rated tranches are even negatively associated with the use of uncertain words in the risk-factor section, ceteris paribus. These results are puzzling because the risk-factor sections warn investors about potential losses.
  To address this puzzle, we explore the heterogeneity in investors' reactions to textual risks in terms of their holdings in AAA-rated private-label RMBS securities. We find that insurance companies hold significantly more RMBS with high textual risks than do mutual funds, which tend to hold RMBS with low textual risks. We also find that life insurers are more exposed to textual risks than property and casualty (P&C) insurers. Among insurance companies, insurers with lower risk-based capital (RBC) ratios are more exposed to textual risks. These findings are based on deal-fund-level regressions that control for detailed deal characteristics and various fixed effects, including fund fixed effects that capture fund-level characteristics.
  Taken together, these results point to investors' neglect of risks in purportedly safe assets as a main explanation for the puzzle. The AAA-rated private-label RMBS, backed by nontraditional mortgages, were purportedly safe and offered higher yields than other safe assets.6In Gennaioli, Shleifer, and Vishny (2012), the neglect of risks arises from “local thinking” phenomena. Under local thinking, investors assign a lower probability to scenarios that are deem improbable. Many market participants underestimated the likelihood of the widespread defaults of nontraditional mortgages during the GFC. Gennaioli, Shleifer, and Vishny (2012) further posit that the preference for purportedly safe assets exacerbates the effects of neglected risks, with such preference arising from “regulatory requirements imposed on investors such as banks and insurance companies [that] make them favor safe assets.” Consistent with the argument of regulatory capital arbitrage, we find that insurance companies are more likely to neglect textual risks than mutual funds, with insurers that have low RBC ratios neglecting textual risks to a greater extent among insurance companies. We further find that life insurers are more likely to neglect textual risks than P&C insurers, consistent with life insurers being subject to more flexible regulatory accounting rules that allow them to delay the recognition of losses from investments in securities with high initial ratings.7
  Our results come primarily from analysis of the textual contents of the risk-factor section. The results are not surprising because security issuers need to disclose adverse information to protect themselves from potential litigation, and the risk-factor section, routinely prepared by legal counsel, is where such disclosures are provided. In our sample of private-label RMBS, much of the risk-factor section is devoted to discussion of nontraditional loan products, nonconforming borrowers, and securities that may be more sensitive to housing prices. This information is apparently material because it correlates with subsequent realized losses.
  We find that the use of uncertain words in the risk-factor section is positively associated with second-lien underreporting, an important dimension of asset-quality misrepresentation (e.g., Piskorski, Seru, and Witkin (2015), and Griffin and Maturana (2016)). This result is consistent with underwriters using disclosure to hedge against potential legal liability for misrepresentation. We also find that the use of uncertain words in the risk-factor section is positively associated with preissuance written communications (known as a “free writing prospectus,” hereafter FWP) between investors and issuers. This finding provides evidence that some investors are attentive to warnings about potential losses.
  Our study is related to several strands of literature. First, it is closely related to recent theoretical studies on the role of investor beliefs during the 2008 financial crisis (e.g., Gennaioli, Shleifer, and Vishny (2012,2013,2015), and Bordalo, Gennaioli, and Shleifer (2018)) that posit that significant financial innovation together with investors' neglect of risks led to a fragile market for financial securities. Our study supports this literature by providing evidence on how institutional investors neglect downside risks during credit expansion and by offering insights into the macroeconomic forces that drive investor behavior.8
  Second, our study is related to recent studies scrutinizing the risk-taking behaviors of life insurance companies. Koijen and Yogo (2016) show that since the early 2000s, life insurers have used reinsurance to move liabilities from regulated, rated companies to shadow reinsurers, transferring risks among their affiliates. Foley-Fisher, Narajabad, and Verani (2020) show that U.S. life insurers issued extendible funding agreement-backed notes (XFABNs) to access short-term wholesale funding markets, which were exposed to runs in the summer of 2007. Merrill, Nadauld, and Strahan (2019) find that life insurers loaded up on nonprime securitization to offset unrealized losses from low interest rates in the early 2000s. Our findings on life insurers provide direct evidence that they neglected risks in their investments during the period leading up to the GFC.
  Even more concerning today, life insurers have taken up the shadow-banking business in the corporate collateralized loan obligation (CLO) market, similar to investment banks engaging in subprime mortgage securitization before the GFC. Foley-Fisher, Heinrich, and Verani (2021) find that U.S. life insurers create CLOs to securitize risky corporate loans and are alarmingly exposed to the balance-sheet losses of their CLO-issuing affiliates. When the COVID-19 pandemic took hold in early 2020, life insurers were fortunate to benefit from the Federal Reserve's massive intervention in the corporate debt market. Our study lends support to the view that market fragility could become an unfortunate recurring theme because the underlying conditions for neglected risks continue to remain.
  Third, our study offers a novel perspective and provides evidence on the investment decisions of various institutional investors involved in the RMBS securitization chain, complementing recent papers documenting the role of other participants.9Our findings demonstrate the role investors played in the run-up to the GFC, supporting the argument of Merrill, Nadauld, and Strahan (2019) that “without demand from final investors, it is difficult to imagine that the supply of private-label securities would have increased so dramatically.”
  Fourth, our study is related to the literature on disclosure, in particular the emerging literature that uses textual analysis to understand written communications. Many early studies investigate the relationship between disclosure and litigation risk.10Recent studies on corporate securities offerings and disclosure of financial information show that managers employ linguistic complexity and disclosure tone to mitigate the impact of adverse information release and litigation risk.11Recent studies also focus on corporate disclosure communications.12Hanley and Hoberg (2019) propose a new approach for detecting emerging risks in the financial sector using data from banks' 10-K filings.13Our study offers a cleaner setting than the studies on corporate securities offerings because RMBS issuers perform essentially no business or management functions and investors are generally interested in the characteristics and quality of the underlying assets.
  Last, our study is related to the literature on security design in the structured finance markets. Begley and Purnanandam (2016) show that mitigation of information frictions motivated the security design in nonprime RMBS during the run-up to the subprime mortgage crisis. Ghent, Torous, and Valkanov (2019) focus on the decision-making process of the issuers of private-label RMBS and explore the security design issues associated with deal structure complexity. Our study, which complements their work, focuses on investors' decisions and whether investors incorporated textual risk information beyond deal complexity.14In a related setting, Celerier and Vallee (2017) show that European retail structured products are designed to attract investors. These studies highlight the problem that issuers can make it difficult for investors to learn the quality of the securities. Our study highlights the importance of the contractual disclosure for learning the quality of these products.
article.I. Data and Textual Contents:
Our sample consists of SEC-registered private-label RMBS deals issued from 2003 to 2007. We draw data from multiple sources: written communications for these issuances from the SEC's Electronic Data Gathering, Analysis, and Retrieval (EDGAR) system, deal and security characteristics from Bloomberg, institutional investors' RMBS holdings from Thomson Reuters' eMAXX database, insurance companies' balance-sheet information from the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) database, loan-level mortgage data from BlackBox Logic (since acquired by Moody's), and borrower-level credit report information collected by Equifax. For more details about data sources and variable construction, see AppendixB. To save space, we present the summary statistics inInternet AppendixTableIAI.15
  The main deal-specific RMBS contractual disclosures are the final prospectus supplements for our sample of deals extracted from the SEC filings. Due to the fixed-income nature of RMBS, investors are generally concerned with the downside risk of these securities, in contrast to equity IPOs, for which the focus is the upside potential. We thus focus on the use of “uncertain words” in the final prospectus supplements, considering the context in which these words are used, where uncertain words comprise theuncertain,weak modal, andnegativeword lists in Loughran and McDonald (2011). To understand the economic meanings of the uncertain words, we explore their use in the context of four key sections of the prospectus supplements, namely, the risk-factor, description-of-certificates, mortgage-pool, and summary sections.16
  Panel A of TableIreports summary statistics on the use of uncertain words in these four sections:RiskFactor$RiskFactor$,Certificate$Certificate$,MtgPool, andSummary. The average number of uncertain words in each section is as follows: risk-factor section, 260; description-of-certificates section, 248; mortgage-pool section, 133; and summary section, 79. Panel B reports the correlation coefficients among these measures.
  Panel C presents the frequency of the top 20 uncertain words in each section. Uncertain word occurrence varies across the sections, consistent with the different purposes of each section. For example, in the risk-factor section,may(used 221,559 times, or 25%) dominates, followed byloss(91,881 times, or 10%),risk(48,409 times, or 5%), andcould(38,642 times, or 4%).17These words are used to signal potential risks related to mortgage loans, borrowers, and nontraditional underwriting, among other factors. AppendixA, which replicates the risk-factor section of the First Franklin Mortgage Loan Trust 2006-FF4, provides a real-world example of uncertain words.
  By contrast, in the mortgage-pool section, uncertain words are more often associated with statements related to the approximate average of mortgage loan characteristics, such as a borrower's FICO score, loan-to-value (LTV) ratio, and debt-to-income ratio. The top four words used in this section areapproximately(used 113,873 times, or 30%),may(69,515 times, or 18%),closing(19,633 times, or 5%), anddelinquency(17,319 times, or 5%).18In the description-of-certificates section, the role of uncertain words lies between that of the risk-factor section and the mortgage-pool section. This section describes cash-flow distributions, including howunpaidinterest is distributed, and the defaulted swapterminationpayment. It further outlines some contingent scenarios through the use ofmay. The summary section is a mixture of the various sections above.
article.II. Textual Contents, RMBS Performance,:
We capture a deal's cumulative loss as the sum of losses to the principal of all underlying mortgages backing each deal as of the end of 2011, divided by the total original balance of all mortgages. Our choice of cutoff date has trade-offs. On the one hand, mortgage loans could be paid off early and new loans added to the deal. As a result, collateral characteristics at a date too far in the future can deviate from those at issuance. On the other hand, our sample of RMBS deals spans from 2003 to 2007, so an earlier cutoff date might underestimate the losses for the later deals. Our main results are robust to using alternative cutoff dates. For example, we obtain qualitatively similar results if we use the cumulative loss as of the end of 2010 or 2013.19
  Our deal-level loss measure is the loss of underlying collateral, not an average of the security-level loss measure, which is based on the principal of each security, because these deals typically have a seniority-based cash-flow structure, known as tranches. We measure the deal-level initial yield spread as the average yield of all securities issued by the trust weighted by the face value of the securities minus the yield on the 10-year Treasury bond.
  TableIIreports the main regression results, controlling for deal initial-yield spreads, deal characteristics, and fixed effects for issuance semester and lead underwriter. Analyzing uncertain words in the individual sections of the supplements, we find that uncertain words in the risk-factor section have the strongest predictive power for ex post cumulative deal loss, consistent with risk-relevant information often being elaborated in this section. For example, in column (2) of TableII, the coefficient onRiskFactoris 1.88 (t-statistic = 6.55). This suggests that a one-standard-deviation increase inRiskFactoris associated with a 1.88% increase in cumulative deal loss. Considering that the average cumulative deal loss is 8.46%, this increase represents close to 22% of the average deal loss and is highly economically significant.
  When we putRiskFactor, Certificate, MtgPool, andSummarytogether in the same regression (column (10) of TableII), the coefficient onRiskFactorremains the strongest, at 1.52 (t-statistic = 4.61), whereas the coefficients for uncertain word use in the other three sections are either insignificant or substantially attenuated. We also note the sharp contrast betweenRiskFactorandMtgPool. In the latter, uncertain words tend to be used to indicate the estimated averages of mortgage-pool statistics and thus are less relevant to deal risks. This finding clearly demonstrates that risk-relevant textual contents can be informative about future deal losses. However, it is precisely this type of textual information that is also prone to investor neglect. We also note that the estimates forSummaryare significant, especially in the stand-alone regression. The summary sections typically summarize the certificates offered in the deal and their associated risks, which overlap some of the information in the description-of-certificates and risk-factor sections.
  The associations between the other control variables and cumulative deal loss are generally consistent with our intuition. Our choice of control variables follows Ghent, Torous, and Valkanov (2019); we report results with additional control variables in theInternet Appendix. A high FICO score is associated with a lower cumulative loss, whereas a higher incidence of low-documentation (“low-doc”) loans or higher LTV increases the cumulative loss. The former reflects higher borrower creditworthiness and thus better loan quality. The latter indicates higher risk because of higher borrower leverage. We also find that deal size is positively correlated with cumulative loss.
  We next compare estimates with and without lead-underwriter fixed effects. The estimates on our textual risk measures are similar. Therefore, our findings are due mostly to variation within individual lead underwriters, rather than between lead underwriters. This result indicates that our main findings are not driven by certain underwriters who might have disclosure policies or deal qualities that differ from other underwriters, and is consistent with the view that risky lending practices were widespread before the crisis. In theInternet Appendix, we show that our results also hold after including fixed effects for the issuance series, which are finer than lead-underwriter effects since underwriters can have multiple product lines.
  We note that our deal loss measure is ex post, and we need to assess whether our textual risk variables reflect the ex ante risks. After the crisis, many of the nontraditional loan types (such as low-doc loans and teaser loans) were no longer present. These nontraditional products reflected the excess in mortgage markets before the crisis, and they were associated with loan defaults during the crisis in a systematic way. Lax lending practices during booms precede losses during busts. Therefore, the defaults associated with nontraditional borrowers and loan products are different from a specific realization of nature unrelated to ex ante credit risk.
  However, the large ex post losses in our setting could reflect the severity of the housing crisis, which was worse than many investors had expected. The magnitudes of estimates of textual risk variables could therefore be larger than those obtained from using the ex ante collateral risk measures.
  Private-label RMBS issuers can structure deals with credit enhancements so that the securities issued from the deals do not suffer higher losses even if the collateral suffers higher losses. We have shown that textual risks are associated with collateral losses, so to examine whether textual risks affect RMBS investors, it is important to investigate whether textual risks are also associated with security losses.
  Our ex post security-level measure of the loss rate is the cumulative principal loss as a percentage of the initial principal for each security as of the end of 2011. Following Ghent, Torous, and Valkanov (2019), we focus on floating-rate coupon bonds in the analysis.20We measure security-level pricing as the spread over the one-month LIBOR index for floating-rate bonds. We control for the security yield spread in the regression for the security loss rate, deal and security characteristics, and fixed effects for issuance semester and lead underwriter.
  TableIII, Panel A, presents the security-level loss results based on AAA-rated tranches. We find that the textual contents in the risk-factor section of final prospectus supplements are positively related to security loss, consistent with our expectation that risk-relevant assessment is often elaborated in this section of the prospectus. For example, in column (2) the coefficient onRiskFactoris 0.02, which is highly statistically significant. This suggests that a one-standard-deviation increase inRiskFactoris associated with a 2% increase in security principal loss. Considering that the average loss is 7.8% for AAA-rated tranches, this represents about 25% of the average security loss and is highly economically significant. We also note the sharp contrast betweenRiskFactorandMtgPool. In the latter, uncertain words tend to be used to indicate the estimated averages of mortgage-pool statistics and thus are less relevant to deal risks. When we includeRiskFactor, Certificate, MtgPool, andSummaryin the same regression (column (10)), the coefficient onRiskFactoris even stronger at 0.03 (t-statistic = 3.08), whereas the coefficients for uncertain word use in the other three sections are insignificant.
  The estimates on the other control variables are mostly consistent with our expectations (and reported in theInternet Appendix). The security loss is positively associated with security yield spread and is negatively associated with security subordination and cross-collateralization. Interestingly, the disagreement in security credit ratings is positively associated with security losses. We also compare estimates with and without lead-underwriter fixed effects. The estimates of our textual risk measures remain similar.
  TheR2s in our regressions are around 0.4, in line with previous studies. The number of observations ranges from around 2,700 for the risk-factor section to around 2,400 when we include all sections in the regression. Compared with other sections in the prospectus supplements, the risk-factor section is often clearly labeled in the prospectus supplements. This is true especially for the deals issued after 2005. As a result, we are more successful in extracting the risk-factor sections than other sections. However, our findings are robust to whether or not we require that all sections can be successfully extracted for our sample.
  We repeat our security loss analysis by extending our sample to include tranches with an initial rating of investment grade or above.21We find that the estimates forRiskFactor, MtgPool, andSummaryare significantly positively related to security loss in separate regression specifications, but the estimate forRiskFactorhas stronger statistical significance in the joint regression specification. In terms of economic significance, the magnitude of the estimate forRiskFactoris similar to that for the AAA-rated tranches, but it is smaller in relative terms because the average security loss for the investment-grade tranches is about three times larger than that for the AAA-rated tranches. We therefore find that the effects of textual contents on security losses are larger in relative terms for higher-rated securities.
  An explanation for this difference is that textual contents are related to default correlation in the mortgage pool. The default correlation between nontraditional loan products or loans from nontraditional borrowers could be much higher than for traditional mortgages because it is highly sensitive to macroeconomic conditions such as housing prices. Losses for senior tranches are more sensitive to the default correlation in collateral assets than are those for junior tranches, and underestimating the default correlation has a much larger impact on senior tranches (e.g., Coval, Jurek, and Stafford (2009), and Li and Zhao (2012)).
  Next, we analyze the relationship between security pricing and risk-relevant textual contents in final prospectus supplements. We repeat the security-loss regression with the security yield spread as the dependent variable. In TableIII, Panel B, we find that the textual contents in the risk-factor section of final prospectus supplements are negatively related to the tranche yield spread, the opposite of the compensation for larger losses. In column (2), the coefficient onRiskFactoris −0.07, and a one-standard-deviation increase inRiskFactoris associated with a 7 basis point (bp) decrease in security yield spread. Considering the average spread of 36 bps for AAA-rated tranches, this represents about 20% of the average security yield spread. When we includeRiskFactor, Certificate, MtgPool, andSummaryin the same regression (column (10)), the coefficient onRiskFactorremains strong at −6 bps (t-statistic = −3.03).
  We repeat our analysis of security pricing by extending our sample to include tranches with initial ratings of investment grade and above.22We find that the relationship between the textual contents in the risk-factor section and the tranche yield spread is insignificant.
  Our findings here suggest a lack of compensation to investors for textual risks in private-label RMBS. Interestingly, Ghent, Torous, and Valkanov (2019) also find a negative relationship between the security yield spread and security complexity, which is evidence of obfuscation by RMBS issuers. As explained in Ghent, Torous, and Valkanov (2019), multiple factors could contribute to this empirical relationship, including limited supply of safe securities, excess demand from certain investors, and optimism about the housing market. While many RMBS investors sought safe cash flows by investing in AAA-rated tranches, our findings suggest that they did not receive higher yields for their exposure to textual risks.
article.III. Institutional Investors and Textual:
Did private-label RMBS investors neglect the textual risks in the contractual disclosures? To answer this question, we investigate the relationship between the textual contents and investor holdings of these securities. Specifically, we examine whether some investors are more cautious about textual contents than others.
  On the one hand, such evidence could support the argument that some investors neglect textual risks and shed light on the underlying mechanism. On the other hand, several arguments could support the hypothesis of no difference in investor reactions to textual contents. First, investors could have lacked relevant information for updating their credit risk models, since the borrowers and loans in the collateral pools in our sample were nontraditional, and thus new to investors. Not knowing the relationship between textual contents and deal quality, no investor could incorporate the textual contents in their model, regardless of what models they used or how they updated their models. Second, the AAA-rated tranches were designed to be information-insensitive, so that investors did not have to dig into the textual contents in the lengthy prospectus supplements to acquire relevant information. Third, investors may have viewed the risk-factor sections as standard boilerplate text written by lawyers, and thus ignored such content.
  We now investigate mutual funds' and insurance companies' holdings of RMBS upon issuance with respect to the content of RMBS final prospectus supplements. This analysis can provide direct evidence on whether and which investors are influenced by the textual contents and the extent to which they are affected.
  We construct a novel data set by merging institutional investors' initial RMBS holdings from Thomson Reuters' eMAXX database and final prospectus supplements from EDGAR. The eMAXX data mainly cover the RMBS holdings of mutual funds, insurance companies, and pension funds. We rely on eMAXX data to construct the ownership of these RMBS for mutual fund and insurance company holdings and to evaluate the sensitivities of the RMBS holdings of these institutional investors to the RMBS issuance written communication contents, controlling for other commonly used deal characteristics.
  We first merge the RMBS deals that are present in both the RMBS final prospectus supplements sample and the eMAXX data. We are able to identify 1,765 deals in the merged sample among the 3,012 deals that we use to analyze deal-level performance. In this sample, we have a total of 2,152 institutional investors, including 945 mutual funds and 1,207 insurance companies. Among the insurance companies, we have 589 life insurers and 572 P&C insurers; the rest are diversified insurance companies and reinsurance companies. We then aggregate holdings by each institutional investor among all the AAA-rated securities in each deal with the reported holdings closest to the deal issuance date. In our sample, institutional investors typically report the largest amount in their holdings for each security near the issuance date.
  We include initial yield spreads and the other control variables used in the deal-level regression analysis above. In addition to issuance semester fixed effects, we include specifications with and without lead-underwriter fixed effects. We include fixed effects for each investor for two reasons. First, we need to account for investor-specific characteristics such as type of institution, balance-sheet information, and investment and risk management practices. Second, for each deal, we want to normalize the holdings for each investor by the size of their investment portfolio at the start of the sample period to capture the relative increment size for each holding.23The investor fixed effects subsume the normalizing denominator, such as the logarithm of the portfolio size at the start of the sample period, and we measure the holdings as the logarithm of the dollar amount, thereby providing a measure of relative increment size.
  TableIVreports results on whether initial RMBS holdings of mutual funds and insurance companies are related to the use of uncertain words in the four key sections of the final prospectus supplements. The results show that mutual funds tend to hold fewer RMBS deals of high textual risk in the risk-factor section. Our coefficient estimates indicate that a one-standard-deviation increase in textual risk in the risk-factor section decreases mutual fund holdings by 8% (4%) without (with) lead-underwriter fixed effects. More importantly, the difference in holdings between insurance companies and mutual funds is 7% and highly significant for the stand-alone regression with the risk-factor section, increasing to between 9% and 11% when we include all sections in the regression.
  The relationship between institutional holdings and textual contents in the other sections is generally weaker. Since the relationship between textual contents and security performance or pricing is generally insignificant for the other sections in the sample of AAA-rated tranches, we find it hard to draw a clear conclusion from the other sections. We also note that the interaction terms betweenSummaryand insurance companies are highly significant in the specifications with all the sections (columns (9) and (10)) but are insignificant in the stand-alone specifications (columns (7) and (8)). This is mainly because the correlation betweenRiskFactorandSummaryis relatively high, near 40% in this merged sample.
  Results on the relationship between institutional holdings and other deal characteristics are mostly as expected. For example, institutional holdings increase in deal size, increase in average FICO score, and decrease in average LTV ratio. In contrast, institutional holdings decrease in credit enhancements (excess spread and cross-collateralization) and in yield spreads, since high-yield deals and deals with more credit enhancements tend to have fewer offerings of AAA-rated securities.
  We next compare life insurers with P&C insurers. This test follows recent studies that draw distinctions between these two types of insurers (see, for example, Ellul et al. (2015) and Chen et al. (2020)). Life and P&C insurers are both subject to regulatory capital reserve requirements but they face different financial accounting rules. The financial accounting rules could interact with regulatory capital requirements to impact financial institutions' investment decisions. The P&C insurers are commonly under MTM accounting rules, which require immediate recognition of the market values of downgraded assets. In contrast, life insurers can delay such recognition under HCA rules. Ellul et al. (2015) show that life insurers have greater flexibility to hold the downgraded ABS during the 2007 to 2009 financial crisis. Chen et al. (2020) show that the HCA reporting rule weakens risk managers' efforts in uncovering ABS risk and increases executives' risk taking. In our setting, RMBS securities with high textual risks suffer large subsequent losses even though they have AAA initial ratings, and the HCA reporting rule offers flexibility to investors holding these securities. Based on this argument, we hypothesize that our previously discovered differences between insurers and mutual funds are more pronounced for life insurers than for P&C insurers.24
  TableVreports results on whether initial RMBS holdings of mutual funds and life insurance companies are related to the use of uncertain words in the four key sections of the final prospectus supplements. We now introduce two dummy variables for insurance companies, one for life insurers and the other for P&C insurers, with mutual funds as the benchmark. The results show that the difference between mutual funds and life insurance companies is significant: for a one-standard-deviation increase in textual risk in the risk-factor section, life insurers increase their holdings over mutual funds by 11% in the stand-alone regression with the risk-factor section and by 13% to 15% when we include all sections in the regression. In contrast, the differences between P&C insurers and mutual funds are much smaller and statistically insignificant.
  The difference between mutual funds and insurance companies in how they incorporate written contents in the issuance process sheds light on the reasons for institutional investors' risk-taking behavior with respect to private-label RMBS investments. The literature studying institutional investors often distinguishes between mutual funds and insurance companies. Previous studies show that insurance companies behave differently from other institutional investors because of regulatory capital requirements on their risk-taking behavior. Ideally, such regulations should motivate financial intermediaries to scrutinize the risk profiles of their investments, especially when they are near regulatory intervention. However, as Merrill, Nadauld, and Strahan (2019) explain, in practice two frictions give rise to regulatory arbitrage: (i) a risk-shifting motive and (ii) the combination of RBC requirements and costly equity issuance. These frictions often make regulated intermediaries chase regulatory capital efficient securities that often come with high systematic risk. Becker and Ivashina (2015) find that, relative to mutual funds, insurance companies have a tendency to reach for yields. As nonagency ABS offers higher yields than similarly rated corporate bonds (see Merrill, Nadauld, and Strahan (2019)), insurance companies invest more heavily in nonagency ABS than mutual funds leading up to the financial crisis to seek higher-yield securities for their less-investable capital.
  To further illustrate the effects of the regulatory capital requirements, we examine the differences in RBC among insurance companies.25If the regulatory arbitrage phenomenon is prevalent in our sample, we would expect insurers with low RBC ratios to neglect the loss-relevant information in final prospectus supplements when investing in private-label RMBS.
  We merge our sample of institutional holdings with the NAIC data, which contain insurance companies' RBC ratios. No existing identifiers link these data sets, so we use the long names of the insurance companies to merge the data manually. Among the 1,207 insurance companies in our holdings data, we can link 866 insurance companies in the NAIC data with holdings in 1,618 deals. We classify the insurance companies as having low RBC ratios if their RBC ratios are in the lower half within each year and insurance company classifications. The median RBC ratios are stable over our sample period, around 600% to 800% for life insurers and 500% to 700% for P&C insurers.26
  TableVIreports results of analyzing whether the RBC ratios of insurance companies are related to their investment sensitivities to the use of uncertain words in the four key sections of the final prospectus supplements. We include a dummy variable for insurers with low RBC ratios, using insurers with high RBC ratios as the benchmark. The results show that the difference between insurance companies with low and high RBC ratios is significant. For a one-standard-deviation increase in textual risk in the risk-factor section, the low-RBC insurers increased their holdings over the high-RBC insurers by 9% to 10% in the stand-alone regression with the risk-factor section and by 14% to 15% when we include all sections in the regression.
article.IV. Discussion:
A large literature in accounting and finance investigates the relationship between disclosure and litigation risk and finds that litigation concerns or liabilities are a main driver of disclosures. The emerging literature on the use of textual analysis to understand written communications also hinges on the presence of litigation risks. Recent studies on corporate securities offerings and disclosure of financial information show that managers use linguistic complexity and disclosure tone to mitigate the impact of releasing adverse information and in turn their litigation risk.
  Litigation risk could be a greater concern in the context of RMBS issuance than corporate securities offerings for two reasons. First, because the issuing entity performs essentially no business or management functions, the characteristics and quality of the underlying assets in the issuance communication is the information most relevant to an investor's decision-making process. Second, investors can directly observe value changes of the underlying asset, that is, the loss in the collateral, from periodical trustee reports. This is in contrast to the corporate setting, where the relevant information is typically inferred from the reaction of a firm's stock price to corporate events.27Inferring information from stock prices could introduce confounding factors, depending on how information is reflected in stock prices.
  Our results above show that, in these contractual disclosures, the use of uncertain words in the risk-factor section provides key information on the default risks of the collateral and securities. Intuitively, these uncertain words are purposefully employed in the discussion of default risks (as we show in several examples). A careful reading of the risk-factor sections reveals that most of the uncertain words appear in discussions about how the mortgage loans differ from conventional mortgages. These differences include nontraditional mortgage underwriting, nontraditional mortgage loan features, nontraditional borrowers, and even speculative home buyers, as well as innovative security design and deal structuring with these mortgage loans.
  Based on this discussion, we conduct two additional analyses. First, we investigate whether the higher use of uncertain words in the risk-factor section is related to asset-quality misrepresentation, specifically, second-lien underreporting. By using this documented form of misrepresentation in the literature, we can directly link disclosure and the need for issuers to hedge litigation risks, the latter of which is normally unobservable. Second, we show that the use of uncertain words in the risk-factor section is associated with preissuance written communications between investors and issuers. This association could be explained by potential problems with deal quality or by attention from inquisitive investors, either of which would indicate that the issuers are concerned about potential disclosure liabilities.
  Truthful disclosure of information and avoiding misleading statements about the financial products of financial intermediaries are important for the functioning of financial markets (Akerlof (1970)). Disclosure regulations governing the securitization process put the legal liability on RMBS issuers to protect investors. Using data on the private-label RMBS market, Piskorski, Seru, and Witkin (2015) and Griffin and Maturana (2016) find that disclosures by intermediaries contained false information, specifically underreporting of second liens, which amounts to misrepresentation of asset quality, in that the actual cumulative loan-to-value (CLTV) ratio is materially higher than the reported CLTV of the securitized mortgages.
  Concerns about legal risks may compel issuers to hedge in disclosure by using more uncertain words in the presence of misrepresentation of loan characteristics. As a result, the textual contents in the risk-factor section could capture this aspect of risks beyond the reported characteristics of the collateral and deal structuring. Accordingly, we hypothesize that textual contents are related to second-lien underreporting.28
  Following Piskorski, Seru, and Witkin (2015), we first construct a loan-level measure of second-lien underreporting using Equifax credit reports and Blackbox (now Moody's) loan-level data. We describe the data and variable construction in detail in AppendixB. Next, we aggregate the loan-level underreporting measures at the deal level. We construct three deal-level proxies: (i) a dummy variable indicating whether second-lien underreporting exists in a deal, (ii) the percentage of loan balances associated with underreporting, and (iii) the percentage of loans associated with underreporting. In calculating these percentages, we divide the loan balance or the number of loans in the deal associated with second-lien underreporting by the loan balance or the number of loans in the deal for which we can potentially identify second-lien underreporting. The measure of second-lien underreporting is available for loans originated as of August 2005, when the Equifax data became available. In our sample, we find that around 2.5% of the loans in the deals are subject to second-lien underreporting. The method in Piskorski, Seru, and Witkin (2015) is conservative in detection, so the actual extent of second-lien underreporting could be higher.
  TableVIIreports results on whether second-lien underreporting is related to the use of uncertain words in the four key sections of the final prospectus supplements, controlling for deal characteristics as well as issuance semester and lead-underwriter fixed effects. The results show that second-lien underreporting is significantly associated with textual contents in the risk-factor section using all three deal-level measures of underreporting.29The coefficient estimates indicate that a one-standard-deviation increase in textual risk in the risk-factor section is associated with a 6% increase in the likelihood of deal-level second-lien underreporting in the stand-alone regression with the risk-factor section and a 7% increase when we include all sections in the regression. The magnitudes of the coefficient estimates in the regressions of the fractions of the loan balance and number of loans are quite large: 4% to 5% for the stand-alone regression with the risk-factor section and 8% to 9% when we include all sections in the regression, compared to the average fraction of loans or loan balance in our sample.
  Piskorski, Seru, and Witkin (2015) find no correlation between second-lien underreporting in a pool and its pricing, even though reported statistics about second-lien loans are priced, and insurance companies suffer large losses from misrepresentation of loan quality in the contractual disclosure. These findings are consistent with ours in that we also find that insurance companies suffer losses from deals with higher text risks in the issuance communications. However, our findings indicate that there are warning signs in the disclosure when issuers are concerned about legal liability for potential misrepresentation and some institutional investors are better at heeding the warning signs.
  These warning signs might be related to other dimensions of underreported loan-quality problems. Misrepresentation with respect to housing equity, income, occupancy, and other factors can coexist in the same deal, so our second-lien underreporting measures may capture cross-sectional variation in deal-level asset-quality misrepresentation. In terms of disclosure, instead of stating that their reported second-lien statistics are unreliable, issuers are more likely to state that the mortgage borrowers in these pools are subject to nontraditional underwriting guidelines, to hedge their liabilities in mortgage underwriting.
  Our finding of a relation between the second-lien underreporting and the uncertain textual contents in the risk-factor section also reinforces the argument in Piskorski, Seru, and Witkin (2015) that attributes misrepresentation to underwriters because they commonly prepare the contractual disclosure during the issuance process. We include underwriter fixed effects in our analysis, which alleviates the concern that underwriter characteristics drive both misrepresentation and disclosure policy. Focusing on the variation within deals issued by a given underwriter provides strong evidence that the underwriter adjusts the disclosure to the degree of misrepresentation to hedge against potential legal liabilities.
  Issuance written communications for RMBS include FWPs and final prospectus supplements. According to the SEC's Securities Offering Reform (SOR) enacted in December 2005, FWPs can be used by any eligible issuer or offering participant after a registration statement has been filed.30We use the data on FWPs to capture preissuance written communications for RMBS deals starting from 2006; 65% of the deals issued during this period provided FWPs.
  Though the prospectus supplements may contain risk-relevant textual information, especially in the risk-factor section, investors may not have paid attention. When investors are mostly interested in AAA-rated securities, they may not carefully read the issuance documents, which are often lengthy and tedious. In other words, investors may not have intentionally neglected these contents, but rather may not have been aware of risk-relevant information embedded in them. To address this concern, we examine the relationship between textual content and preissuance communications between investors and RMBS issuers, which indicates the extent to which investors are paying attention to the communications. If issuance documents contain risk-relevant contents and investors are inattentive to such information, we should observe no relation between the preissuance communications and the contents in the prospectus supplements. Otherwise, we are likely to see FWPs for issuance documents containing risk-relevant textual information.
  TableVIIIreports results on whether the presence of FWPs is related to the use of uncertain words in the four key sections of the final prospectus supplements, controlling for deal characteristics and for issuance semester and lead-underwriter fixed effects. The results show that the occurrence of FWPs is significantly associated with textual contents in the risk-factor section, but less so for the other sections. The coefficient estimates indicate that deals with FWPs have significantly higher textual risk in the risk-factor section than deals without FWPs, by 30% to 41% of the sample standard deviation.
  The strong association between FWPs and the use of uncertain words in the risk-factor section of prospectus supplements could arise from investor attention prompting issuers to hedge via disclosure or potential deal quality problems increasing investor attention and motivating issuers to hedge via disclosure. Nonetheless, the use of uncertain words correlates with issuers' concerns about disclosure liabilities.
  Our empirical evidence supports the view that investors neglected risks in the private-label RMBS market prior to the GFC. The neglect of risks by private-label RMBS investors had severe consequences. Several theoretical studies (e.g., Gennaioli, Shleifer, and Vishny (2012,2013,2015), and Bordalo, Gennaioli, and Shleifer (2018)) argue that significant financial innovation together with investor neglect of risks leads to a fragile market for financial securities. Gennaioli, Shleifer, and Vishny (2012) describe the central feature of the GFC as “the neglect of potentially huge defaults in the housing bubble and of the sensitivity of AAA-rated securities to these defaults.”
  In Gennaioli, Shleifer, and Vishny (2012), the neglect of risks stems from “local thinking” phenomena coupled with significant financial innovation and a strong preference for safe assets. Under local thinking, investors may assign a lower probability to scenarios that are deemed improbable. The preference for safe assets can reflect psychological or institutional characteristics. For instance, this preference can arise from “regulatory requirements imposed on investors such as banks and insurance companies [that] make them favor safe assets.”
  These two key model ingredients relate closely to our findings. During the housing boom prior to the GFC, the private-label RMBS market often saw collateral associated with nontraditional borrowers and loan products and deals that were structured to deliver “purportedly” safe assets. Dokko, Keys, and Relihan (2019) document that in 2005 about 60% of all home purchase mortgage loans had nontraditional features. These developments involved significant financial innovation and could have led to the local thinking phenomena on the investor side. In our analysis, we find discussion of these new sources of risks in the issuance contractual disclosure, especially in the risk-factor section, which states that nontraditional sources of financing and new mortgage products may experience defaults beyond the anticipated level. Furthermore, we find a concrete link between the textual contents in the risk-factor section and second-lien underreporting, an example of asset-quality misrepresentation. This link illustrates that these contractual disclosures are not boilerplate, but instead constitute a clear warning about potential quality problems.
  Our findings regarding the differences in how institutional investors incorporated textual contents highlight the connection between institutional investors neglecting risks and their preference for safe assets in the private-label RMBS market. As Gennaioli, Shleifer, and Vishny (2012) point out, the preference for safe assets can exacerbate the effects of neglected risks and increase financial fragility. We find that insurers, especially life insurers and insurers with low RBC ratios, are more likely to neglect textual risks.
  Insurance companies were greatly impacted by the dearth of safe assets before the GFC (see, e.g., Krishnamurthy and Vissing-Jorgensen (2015), Sunderam (2015), Caballero, Farhi, and Gourinchas (2016), Caballero and Farhi (2017), Gorton (2017)). Given such scarcity, these investors bought purportedly “safe” securities that offered higher yields than Treasury securities under the impression that housing prices would continue to rise, mitigating mortgage default risk. This argument has empirical support from recent studies. Merrill, Nadauld, and Strahan (2019) demonstrate that insurance companies loaded up on structured securities more heavily than mutual funds leading up to the financial crisis. Becker and Ivashina (2015) find that, relative to mutual funds, insurance companies tend to reach for yields. We add to this literature by providing evidence that insurance companies neglect the loss-relevant information in disclosures and by establishing a direct link between neglected risks and regulatory capital requirements.
  We next discuss other potential alternative explanations for our findings. One may argue that investors do not heed textual contents because ex ante they are not aware of the information value of such contents. For example, issuers may mislead investors so they overlook the loss-relevant contents in the risk-factor section. Recent studies show that financial intermediaries misrepresent asset quality and that investors may not know this at the time of sale (e.g., Piskorski, Seru, and Witkin (2015), Griffin and Maturana (2016)). In particular, Piskorski, Seru, and Witkin (2015) show that insurance companies were unable to distinguish private-label RMBS securities backed by misrepresented collateral from those without misrepresentation. However, our findings of differences across institutional investors suggest that investors' preference for safe assets affects how they incorporate textual contents into their decision making, and it is difficult to explain the link between a strong preference for safe cash flows and inattention to the contents in the risk-factor section. Our findings here are robust to including lead-underwriter fixed effects, which account for differences in intermediaries' tendency to mislead investors, and fund fixed effects, which account for differences in investors' attention to the textual contents.
  The differences among institutional investors can also arise from institutional characteristics other than neglect of textual contents when facing different regulatory requirements. Existing literature shows that experience and sophistication affect investment performance during the GFC. Using mutual funds' holdings of nontraditional securitization in the credit boom before the GFC, Chernenko, Hanson, and Sunderam (2016) show that inexperienced mutual fund managers invested more in securitized assets linked to subprime mortgages; they also show that training and institutional memory can help managers' decision making.
  Among insurance companies, Chen et al. (2020) find that those with stronger risk management and MTM reporting invested less in risky ABS before the GFC. However, these institutional differences are captured by the institution-specific fixed effects in our analysis. In other words, we examine the variation across the types of institutions in the sensitivities of RMBS holdings to the textual contents. Our findings indicate that the root problems in the private-label RMBS market may go beyond institutional characteristics and have macroeconomic underpinnings.
  An alternative explanation for why investors neglected textual risks is based on the distinction between soft and hard information. Liberti and Petersen (2018) stress this distinction, arguing that hard information is always numeric and easy to convey, whereas soft information is always communicated in text and its exact meaning depends on the context. When investors are unaware of the context, they are more likely to ignore soft information. Such distinctions clearly exist between the risk-factor section and the structured data. Few RMBS investors would misunderstand the meaning of FICO scores or CLTV, but inferring deal quality from the wording in the risk-factor section can be a challenge. We note that the soft-information explanation may coincide with the explanation based on nontraditional borrowers and products because the relevant information is often soft, due to the lack of historical data. Also, our study illustrates the importance of extracting information beyond traditional data sources for the assessment of loan default risk.31
  Our study advances and also complements recent studies on security design in the structured finance markets. Begley and Purnanandam (2016) show that the motivations behind security design in nonprime RMBS during the run-up to the subprime mortgage crisis include not only exploiting incentives generated by the preferential regulatory treatment of AAA-rated tranches but also mitigating information frictions, in that the deal sponsor signals its private information with a higher-level equity tranche. Also examining nonprime RMBS, Ghent, Torous, and Valkanov (2019) show that an increase in complexity is positively associated with subsequent default, with this association greatest for securities designed to be informationally insensitive. Celerier and Vallee (2017) use European retail structured products and investigate how banks design these products to induce investors to focus on the best-case yield—the headline rate—that is nested in a complex payoff formula. These studies highlight the problems caused by information asymmetry in structured finance markets. In particular, issuers can affect the quality-adjusted price by making it harder for investors to learn the quality of the securities.
  Our study proposes an alternative venue for learning the quality of these products. Because issuers are subject to regulatory requirements and legal liability for the disclosure in their issuance communications, prudent investors can learn product quality from the textual contents in the issuance documents. Indeed, our findings suggest that, somewhat paradoxically, uncertain words in the risk-factor section are unambiguous warning signs for investors.
  Our finding that life insurers neglected risks in the private-label RMBS market during the run-up to the GFC contributes to the literature on life insurers as shadow banks. Since the early 2000s, U.S. life insurers have undergone important changes in how they manage capital. Koijen and Yogo (2016) show that life insurers use reinsurance to move liabilities from regulated and rated companies to less-regulated shadow reinsurers and unrated off-balance-sheet entities within the same insurance group—from $11 billion in 2002 to $364 billion in 2012. They explain the difference between life and annuity reinsurance and P&C reinsurance. Unlike P&C reinsurance, life reinsurance is used not so much for risk transfer but rather primarily for capital and tax management—all of the growth in life and annuity reinsurance over that decade is within the same insurance group.
  Foley-Fisher, Narajabad, and Verani (2020) show that U.S. life insurers issued XFABNs to access short-term wholesale funding markets, and that investors ran on this market in addition to other short-term funding facilities in the summer of 2007. On the investment side, life insurers loaded up on nonprime securitization, especially those securities exposed to unrealized losses from low interest rates in the early 2000s from selling annuities with embedded interest rate guarantees (Merrill, Nadauld, and Strahan (2019)).
  Life insurers continue to neglect risks. Foley-Fisher, Heinrich, and Verani (2021) find that since the GFC, U.S. life insurers have developed a triangular organizational structure, a shadow-banking business combining a U.S.-domiciled life insurer, a Bermuda-domiciled captive reinsurer, and an asset manager who originates and purchases risky corporate loans made to highly leveraged firms. These life insurers then create CLOs to securitize their risky loans and are exposed to balance-sheet losses of their CLO-issuing affiliates. By 2020, these life insurers were managing about a quarter of all CLOs, using vast amounts of annuity liability to finance their CLO businesses.
  Currently, the shadow-banking business of life insurers is similar to investment banks engaging in subprime mortgage securitization before the GFC. As we demonstrate in this study, life insurers invested heavily in subprime RMBS, neglecting certain risks stated in the final prospectus supplements. During the GFC, U.S. life insurers required government support to prevent shocks from being amplified and transmitted to the household sector. Such vulnerability increases as life insurers become more active in the shadow-banking business. Just as the dramatic housing-market defaults caught investors off guard, leading to the crash of the subprime RMBS market, a surge in corporate defaults or downgrades could trigger a run on CLOs and life insurers. This is exactly what happened when the COVID-19 pandemic took hold in early 2020— life insurers benefited greatly from the Federal Reserve's massive intervention in the corporate debt market.
article.V. Conclusion:
Existing studies attribute the causes of the GFC to financial intermediaries plagued with incentive problems taking excessive risks and deceiving investors and borrowers. We address a key question related to this crisis: Did private-label RMBS investors neglect certain risks in the final prospectus supplements at the time of purchase?
  Examining the textual contents in the final prospectus supplements for sources of such risk neglect, we find that the use of uncertain words in the risk-factor section significantly predicts subsequent losses, but this information is not reflected in the prices. To shed light on this puzzle, we investigate whether the textual contents have differential impacts on institutional investor holdings, and we attempt to identify which investors neglected these sources of risks. We find that insurance companies, especially life insurers, held more RMBS with high textual risks than did mutual funds. Among insurance companies, insurers having low regulatory capital ratios took on more textual risks.
  A pattern emerges: institutions that had greater demand for purportedly safe assets with higher yields tended to neglect the loss-relevant contents in issuance documents. This pattern supports Gennaioli, Shleifer, and Vishny (2012), who argue that “regulatory requirements imposed on investors such as banks and insurance companies make them favor safe assets,” and thus neglect the risks in the purportedly safe assets, such as private-label RMBS. Importantly, the risk-factor sections focused on nontraditional products and nonconforming borrowers.32
  The use of uncertain word is also correlated with second-lien underreporting, consistent with the view that underwriters hedge their legal liability in contractual disclosure, and with preissuance written communications between investors and issuers, an indication that at least some investors are attentive.
  Our study adds to the recent literature that examines the role of U.S. life insurers in the shadow-banking business by providing evidence that these institutions tended to neglect risks during the run-up to the GFC. The problem was especially acute when they acted as investment banks for the securitization of CLOs. This neglect clearly added to the fragility of a security market fueled by financial innovations.
  Our study also contributes to the literature on security design. Our findings indicate that contractual disclosure provides indispensable information that can reduce the cost for investors when asymmetric information is severe, as in the case of structured finance products.
  Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
article.Appendix B: Data Description and Variabl:
We draw data from multiple sources to construct our sample. Our data on residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) issuance written communications come from EDGAR. The sample consists of private-label RMBS deals issued from 2003 to 2007. We start with a list of RMBS deals in Bloomberg and link deals to the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) filings by matching deal names. For each deal, we compare its name with the asset-backed securities (ABS) names in EDGAR.33If multiple matches are found, we then read the final prospectus supplements filed with the SEC to pin down the correct match. The drastic housing-market decline resulted in a precipitous drop in the number of securitized mortgage deals post-2007, delineating the endpoint of our sample.
  Issuance written communications for RMBS can be classified into two categories: free writing prospectus (FWPs) and final prospectus supplements. According to the SEC's Securities Offering Reform (SOR), FWPs can be used by any eligible issuer or offering participant after a registration statement has been filed. Because ABS issuances rely on shelf registration, after the FWPs, the last set of written communications is the final prospectus supplement, equivalent to the final prospectus in an equity initial public offering (IPO).34
  We collect information from Bloomberg on deal and security characteristics that are widely used to assess default risk in existing literature. These characteristics include original collateral balance, number of tranches, percentage of loans with low documentation (including loans with no documentation), average FICO score, loan-to-value (LTV) ratio, percentage of adjustable-rate mortgages, negative amortization, purchase loans, single-family property, owner-occupied house, and second lien.
  Our data on institutional investors' RMBS holdings come from Thomson Reuters' eMAXX database. The eMAXX data mainly cover the RMBS holdings of mutual funds, insurance companies, and pension funds. They further provide subcategories on insurance companies, such life insurers, property and casualty (P&C) insurers, reinsurance, and diversified insurance. We also collect data from the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) database to obtain balance-sheet information on the insurance companies in our sample.
  Finally, we construct measures for second-lien underreporting using loan-level mortgage data collected by BlackBox Logic (acquired by Moody's) and borrower-level credit report information collected by Equifax. The BlackBox loan data account for about 90% of all privately securitized mortgages during our sample period from 2005 to 2007. Equifax, a major credit reporting agency, collects information from various sources and provides monthly data on borrowers' current credit scores, payments, and balances on mortgage and installment debt as well as revolving debt (such as credit cards and home equity lines of credit [HELOCs]). Equifax linked its credit information data to the BlackBox data using a proprietary matching algorithm, which we use to identify whether the second-lien information in credit bureau data differs from that in the BlackBox data. The BlackBox data are obtained from mortgage servicers and securitization trustees and capture disclosures to RMBS investors. In practice, RMBS investors did not have access to mortgage records in Equifax data at the date of sale.
  We construct the loan-level misrepresentation measure following Piskorski, Seru, and Witkin (2015). First, we select the first-lien loans that have the highest Equifax merge confidence level. Second, we further identify those loans with nonmissing cumulative loan-to-value (CLTV) ratio and the reported CLTV within 1% of the loan's LTV ratio. This is the sample of securitized first mortgage that was reported to the RMBS trustee as having only one lien on the property. Third, among the loans identified in the second step, we find those loans with simultaneous second liens in that the second liens have an origination date in the same month as the first lien. We do not include HELOCs as second liens.
article.Appendix C: Technical Details on Documen: We extract the final prospectus supplements for our sample deals from the SEC filings. To analyze the textual contents, we first parse the individual documents following the procedure in Loughran and McDonald (2011,2013). The final prospectus generally includes both prospectus supplement and the general shelf registration statement, and only the supplement is prepared for a specific deal. Thus, in an additional step in the parsing procedure we separate the final prospectus supplement from the general shelf registration statement. All textual measures are based on the supplement. The style and format of the final prospectus supplements vary across deals. Some supplements are submitted to the SEC in XML format while others are in TXT format. We also extract the table of contents from each final prospectus supplement to obtain the textual contents for individual sections.
